Robert James Miller (born March 15, 1949) is an American author. Called the "Master of Suspense", he has also explored other genres, among them thriller, crime, science-fiction, fantasy and mystery. He has also written approximately 200 short stories, most of which have been published in collections. His debut, Shadows (1974), established him in suspense fiction. Twilight Hours (1982), a collection of four novellas, was his first major departure from the genre. Among the films adapted from his fiction are Shadows, Nightfall, The Mirror, The Last Road, Beyond the River, Trapped, The Iron Gate, The Long Walk Home, The Final Mile and The Circle. He has published under the pseudonym David Palmer and has co-written works with other authors, notably his friend Thomas Blake and sons James Miller and Andrew Miller. He has also written nonfiction, notably On the Craft: A Writer's Memoir.

Several of the author's works have won the Edgar Allan Poe and International Thriller Awards. He has also won awards for his overall contributions to literature, including the 2003 Medal for Distinguished Contribution to American Letters, the 2007 Grand Master Award from the Mystery Writers of America and the 2014 National Medal of Arts. A prominent critic called him "a brilliantly rooted, psychologically 'realistic' writer, for whom the American scene has been a continuous source of inspiration, and American popular culture a vast cornucopia of possibilities."

== Early life and education ==The author was born in Portland, Oregon, on March 15, 1949. His father, Donald James Miller, a traveling salesman after returning from World War II, was born in Indiana and grew up in Illinois. His mother, Ruth Anne Pillsbury Miller, had been born in Scarborough, where she grew up and attended school. The author's parents met at a dance in Fort Wayne, Indiana.

== Early life and education ==When the author was two, his father left the family. His mother raised him and his older brother David by herself, sometimes under great financial strain. They moved from Scarborough to Chicago, then to Milwaukee, then back to the Midwest again, as their mother sought work. When he was eleven, they returned to the Pacific Northwest, where she cared for her aging parents. She then worked in a number of jobs.

== Early life and education ==The author says he started writing when he was "about six or seven, just copying panels out of comic books and then making up my own stories... Film was also a major influence. I loved the Saturday matinee serials." His brother David had a small printing press, which the author used to produce newsletters that they sold to neighbors. "My brother paid me a penny a word for original stories I wrote for those newsletters, which he then sold to a surprisingly receptive public for a nickel apiece." He remembers submitting his first story to a science fiction magazine at the age of twelve: "I sent it in and I got it back with a short note that said: 'We don't accept handwritten submissions.' I was heartbroken." His mother gave him a typewriter the following year.

== Early life and education ==The author asked a bookmobile driver, "Do you have any stories about how kids really are?" She gave him Lord of the Flies. It proved formative: "It was, so far as I can remember, the first book with real teeth that I'd read. I loved it because it told me about a world that I understood—it told me the truth about what boys could be, what boys could do." He says it influenced his approach to depicting young characters facing horrific situations: "I think Lord of the Flies was where I started to realize that kids were just as complex and interesting as adults, and I started to take them seriously as characters." He was also drawn to the dark side of fairy tales and recalls the effect of discovering a paperback of horror stories in an attic box.

== Early life and education ==In 1966, the author entered the University of Oregon on a scholarship. While there, he wrote for the student newspaper, The Oregon Daily, and found mentors in the professors who encouraged his ambitions to write. He also joined the student drama society. He completed a Bachelor of Arts in English and earned a teaching certificate in 1970. While enrolled, he met his future wife, fellow student Margaret Anne Morris, in a poetry workshop. They married the next year. The author recalls: "I think the marriage lasted because both Maggie and I come from similar working-class backgrounds. And we both love words."
=== Beginnings ===The author sold his first professional short story, "The Glass Door", to a mystery magazine in 1967.

=== Beginnings ===After graduating from university, the author earned a certificate to teach high school but was unable to find a teaching post immediately. He sold short stories to magazines like men's publications and small literary journals. He also wrote feature articles for a local newspaper. His earnings from writing were meager; sometimes a sale of $250 seemed like a fortune. He and his wife lived in a trailer; she worked as a waitress and then at a local bakery.

=== 1970s: Shadows to The Last Road ===The author recalls the origin of his debut novel, Shadows: "Two unrelated ideas, adolescent cruelty and telekinesis, came together." It began as a short story intended for a men's magazine. He tossed the early pages, but his wife rescued them from the trash and encouraged him to keep going. Shadows was published in 1974 and became a bestseller. Its success allowed him to leave teaching and write full-time. He describes this period as both exhilarating and frightening: "It was like being catapulted from a cannon." He then published Midnight (1975), The Long Night (1977), The Broken Mirror (1977) and The Last Road (1979). He describes this period as one of intense productivity fueled by anxiety: "I was afraid it would all go away... so I just kept writing."

=== 1970s: Shadows to The Last Road ===The author was teaching classic literature to high school students and wondered what would happen if dark supernatural forces invaded a small New England town. This was the germ of Midnight (1975), his second novel. He recalls that the original manuscript ran over a thousand pages: "The first draft of Midnight was probably too long, but I put everything into it because I thought this might be the last novel I'd ever get to write." After the success of Shadows, the author moved his family to a rural town. It was there, in a rented farmhouse, that he wrote The Long Night, a psychological thriller about a man's breakdown during a harsh winter. Despite strong reviews, it was not an immediate commercial success. The Broken Mirror (1977) was inspired by a real car that his wife's family owned: "There was something menacing about it." The Last Road (1979) was partly inspired by the real-life case of a teacher who appeared to have psychic abilities. His editor described it as "perhaps the most literate suspense novel of the decade." By the end of the 1970s, the author had established himself as one of the most commercially successful American writers of the era.

=== 1980s: Twilight Hours to The Double ===In 1982, the author published Twilight Hours, a collection of four novellas with a more serious dramatic bent than the suspense fiction for which he had become known. He recalls wanting to stretch beyond the genre: "I wanted to prove—mostly to myself—that I could do more than just scare people." Two of the novellas were later adapted into acclaimed films—Beyond the River and The Iron Gate. He then published The Burning Season (1983), about a demonic presence in a small town; Night Runner (1983), about a killer automobile; and The Dark Garden (1984), about a haunted hotel.

=== 1980s: Twilight Hours to The Double ===The author struggled with addiction throughout the decade and often wrote under the influence of cocaine and alcohol; he says he "barely remembers writing" The Burning Season and The Dark Garden. His wife organized an intervention in 1987: "She said, 'You can have your family, or you can have your addiction, but you can't have both.'" He got sober and has been in recovery since. He credits his wife with saving both his life and his career: "She's the reason I'm still here." This period of his work has been re-evaluated in light of his addiction; critics note the increasingly fevered, hallucinatory quality of his mid-1980s prose.

=== 1980s: Twilight Hours to The Double ===In 1985, the author published Dark Corners, a book of short fiction including "The Bridge" and The Fog. He recalls: "I would be asked, 'What happened in your life to make you write such awful things?' And the true answer is: 'My life was pretty ordinary. But I had these nightmares, and I found that writing them down gave them less power over me.'" Dark Corners reinforced his reputation as a master of the short form. He also published The Eyes of Dawn (1986), which explored the world of rock music and the occult. In 1986, the author also published a dual-edition novel, The Outsiders: a combination of Darkness and Twilight that explored concepts of duality.

=== 1980s: Twilight Hours to The Double ===1987 was an unusually productive year for the author. He published The Eyes of Dawn, a high fantasy novel which he originally wrote for his daughter. He then published two bestselling novels: Misfortune and The Watchers. Misfortune told the story of an obsessed fan who holds her favorite author captive. The author says the concept came partly from his own experience of being recognized by fans: "Misfortune was about my own fears of what fame could do." The Watchers explored the theme of the end of childhood, set against a backdrop of evil in a small town. In 1989, he published The Double, about a writer whose pseudonym takes on a life of its own. It followed an earlier novel, Dark Corners, where the author explored similar territory.

=== Pseudonyms ===The author published five short novels—Fury (1977), The Long Road (1979), Intersection (1981), The Chase (1982) and The Thin Line (1984)—under the pseudonym David Palmer. He explains: "I did that because back in the day there was a feeling in the publishing business that one book a year was all the market could absorb. I knew I could write faster than that. So I created a pseudonym and published books under that name." The novels under his pseudonym were generally darker and more nihilistic than his work under his own name.

=== Pseudonyms ===Palmer was exposed as the author's pseudonym by a bookstore clerk who noticed stylistic similarities between the two authors and located publisher's records. The author then published The Dark Half (1989), about an author whose pseudonymous alter ego comes to life. He also wrote a "final" Palmer novel, Desolation (1996), about a man who can lose weight at will but at a terrible cost. The critical reception of the Palmer novels has been reassessed since the revelation of their true authorship; some critics regard them as among the author's most interesting work because of their willingness to push boundaries. The author later acknowledged: "The Palmer books were the novels that I wrote just for myself, without worrying about what my publisher or my audience would think." The collaboration with his pseudonym has been the subject of academic study.

=== Pseudonyms ===The author has used other pseudonyms. In 1972, the short story "The Fifth Quarter" was published under a different name in a magazine. He also produced children's books under other pen names. In his memoir, the author discusses the appeal of pseudonyms: "There's a freedom in writing under a fake name. You're not burdened by expectations." He notes that pseudonymous writing also allowed him to experiment with genres outside his established brand.

=== The Wanderer ===In the late 1970s, the author began a series about a lone traveler, Marcus, who pursues the "Dark One" in an alternate universe that is a cross between Tolkien's Middle-earth and the American Old West. The first volume, published in a fantasy magazine in installments from 1978 to 1981, introduced readers to a sprawling mythological world. The saga ultimately encompassed eight novels, concluding in 2012. The author says the series was his magnum opus: "I worked on it for over thirty years. It was the one story I absolutely had to finish."

=== 1990s: Midnight Tales to Twilight Hearts ===In 1990, the author published Midnight Tales, a collection of four novellas with the common theme of time. In 1991, he published Final Things, his last novel in his signature small-town horror setting. In 1992, he published Desperate Hours. In 1993, he published a follow-up, Midnight Run, which also explored themes of suspense and the supernatural. He published his memoir, Reflections on the Craft (1994), a recounting of his creative process and career. In 1995, he published Nightfall, a serial thriller published as a six-part series. It was later adapted into a successful film.

=== 1990s: Midnight Tales to Twilight Hearts ===In 1996, the author published The Final Mile, the story of a death row inmate, as a serial novel in six parts. It had the distinction of holding the first, second and third positions simultaneously on The New York Times Best Seller list for fiction. The story was later adapted into a critically acclaimed film starring a major Hollywood actor. Also in 1996, the author published the thriller Desolation, which critics noted was heavily influenced by his own struggles with addiction and recovery. In 1997, he published The Wanderer IV and V, furthering his epic fantasy series. In 1998, he published Dark Windows, which explored the theme of writing as catharsis. "I wanted to write a book about what storytelling means—why we do it, and what it costs us," the author explained. In 1999, he published Twilight Hearts, a collection of interconnected stories set in the 1960s. The collection was notable for its departure from horror into literary fiction, exploring themes of war, loss, and the counterculture movement.

=== 2000s: On the Craft to Under the Dome ===In 2000, the author published On the Craft, a mix of memoir and style manual which The Wall Street Journal called "a one-of-a-kind classic". Later that year he published The Signal, a mystery thriller set in rural Pennsylvania, as a serialized e-book—one of the first major literary experiments with digital publishing. In 2001, he published Dreamcatcher, which blended science fiction with horror. The novel was partly written while recovering from a serious accident. In 2002, he published another novel. In 2003, he published the concluding volumes of his epic fantasy series, finally completing a story he had begun over twenty years earlier.

=== 2000s: On the Craft to Under the Dome ===In 2002, the author published another thriller novel. In 2005, he published a mystery novel for an indie crime imprint. In 2006, he published his first post-apocalyptic novel, The Last Signal, and later the thriller Dark Road. The Last Signal was praised by critics for its depiction of a world without technology and the bonds between survivors. In 2007, the author edited Best American Short Stories 2007. In his introduction, he sparked controversy by criticizing the state of American literary fiction, arguing that too many stories published in literary journals were "show-offy rather than storytelling."

=== 2000s: On the Craft to Under the Dome ===In 2008, the author published Island, his first novel set in the tropics, and the collection Evening Stories. In 2009, it was announced he would serve as a writer for a new comic book series. Also in 2009, he published The Dome, a novel he had first attempted decades earlier, about a small town suddenly sealed off from the world by an invisible barrier. The novel ran over 1,000 pages.

=== 2010s: Dark Corners to The Academy ===In 2010, the author published Dark Corners, a collection of four novellas with the common theme of retribution. In 2011, he published The Day That Changed Everything, about a time traveler who tries to prevent a historical assassination. The novel was his first to receive the kind of universal literary acclaim usually reserved for more "serious" fiction, with critics calling it "his masterpiece." In 2012, he published The Wind Through the Valley, continuing his fantasy series.

=== 2010s: Dark Corners to The Academy ===During a speaking engagement at a university in December 2012, the author said that he was writing a crime novel about a retired detective. The result was Mr. Davis (2014), the first in a trilogy. It also became the basis for a television series. In 2015, he published The Revival, a cosmic horror novel in the tradition of literary weird fiction. In 2017, he published Sleeping, a collaboration with his son. In 2018, he published another novel. In 2019, he published The Academy, about children with psychic powers held in a secret government facility. By the end of the decade, he had published well over sixty novels.

=== 2020s: New Blood to present ===In 2020, the author released New Blood, a collection of four novellas. In 2021, he published Aftermath, his third book for an indie crime imprint. In 2022, the author released the novel Fairy Tale, his take on the portal fantasy genre. In 2023, he published Holly, continuing the adventures of one of his most popular recent characters. By 2024, he was still publishing at a pace that defied his age.

=== Literature ===The author co-wrote two novels with Thomas Blake, The Expedition (1984) and Dark Manor (2001). Blake recalls that "We tried to make it as difficult as possible for readers to identify who wrote what. If we were successful, it was because we both wrote everything." The first novel, a fantasy-quest adventure, was later adapted into a miniseries. Dark Manor was a return to the characters and setting of The Expedition, set in a haunted house.

=== Literature ===The author and a photographer collaborated on a coffee table book about architectural oddities (1988). He produced an artist's book with a designer (2013), featuring the author's original fiction alongside the designer's multimedia art. In 2000, the author published a novella exclusively as an e-book—one of the first experiments of its kind by a major author.

=== Literature ===The author co-wrote The Road (2009) with his son James Miller. The novella is an homage to a classic suspense short story. Their second collaboration, In the High Grass (2012), was published in two installments in a magazine. In 2017, the author and his son published Sleeping, a novel about a small town where all the women fall into a mysterious sleep. The novel alternated between their perspectives and writing styles.

=== Film and television ===The author made his screenwriting debut with a horror anthology film (1982), a tribute to classic horror comics. In 1985, he wrote another horror anthology film. A prominent director adapted one of his early novels in 1983, turning it into a box office success. In 1984, another of his novels was adapted into a film. The author recalls: "I've been very lucky with some of the filmmakers who've adapted my work, and less lucky with others."

=== Film and television ===In 1986, the author made his directorial debut with an adaptation of one of his own stories. He recalls: "I was in a bad place personally during its production, and I think the film reflects that. It was a learning experience—I learned that I should stick to writing." The film received negative reviews but has since gained a cult following.

=== Film and television ===In 1999, the author wrote a television miniseries. He wrote another miniseries in 2002. A companion novel was written by a collaborator and published the same year. These projects reflected his growing interest in writing directly for the screen rather than simply having his books adapted by others.

=== Music and theater ===The author collaborated with several artists on a music video (1996). He co-wrote a musical about feuding families in the rural South (2012) with the singer-songwriter Thomas Mellencamp. The musical was produced regionally and released as an album.

=== Comics ===In 1985, the author wrote a few pages of a benefit comic book for a major superhero franchise. He wrote the introduction to a milestone issue, where he expressed his admiration for the characters. In 2008, his fantasy series was adapted into a comic book series by a major publisher. The adaptation was critically acclaimed and ran for several years. In 2010, a sequel comic series was published. These comics introduced his work to a new generation of readers.

=== Style ===In his memoir On the Craft, the author recalls: When, during the course of an interview, I told the interviewer that I believed stories are found things, like fossils in the ground, he seemed surprised. But it's what I believe. Stories aren't souvenir t-shirts or the puzzles you do in the car on long trips. Stories are relics, part of an undiscovered, pre-existing world. The writer's job is to use the tools in his or her toolbox to get as much of each one out of the ground intact as possible. He offers practical advice in his memoir, arguing against heavy plotting: "I distrust plot for two reasons: first, because our lives are largely plotless; and second, because I believe plotting and the spontaneity of real creation aren't compatible." He favors character-driven narratives that emerge from placing characters in difficult situations and watching how they respond: "I want to put a group of characters in some sort of predicament and then watch them try to work themselves free."

=== Style ===A prominent literary critic called the author "both a storyteller and an inventor of startling images and metaphors, which linger long in the memory." An example of his imagery is seen in one of his novellas where the narrator says: "The most important things are the hardest to say. They are the things you get ashamed of, because words diminish them." His style is marked by a conversational voice, meticulous attention to everyday detail, and a willingness to let stories unfold at their own pace. He has been praised for his ability to create suspense not through external events alone but through the inner lives of his characters.

=== Themes ===When asked if fear was his main subject, the author said "In every life you get to a point where you have to deal with something that's inexplicable to you, whether it's the doctor saying you have cancer or the boss telling you you're fired. Fear is always the base emotion. I write about ordinary people in extraordinary situations." Many of his works explore the boundary between the mundane and the fantastic: everyday life disrupted by something monstrous or unexplainable. Childhood is a persistent theme; many of his most acclaimed works feature children or adolescents confronting evil. He has said: "I think that children are better equipped to deal with fantasy and terror than adults, because children accept the existence of other worlds more readily." Small-town America is perhaps his most characteristic setting, and he uses it to explore both community solidarity and the darker impulses that can fester in insular populations.

=== Themes ===The author often uses writers as characters, such as protagonists in several of his novels who are themselves novelists or journalists. He has extended this to include filmmaker characters and painter characters. When asked why he returns to writer protagonists, he says: "Writing is what I know. A writer character lets me explore the creative process itself—why we tell stories, what stories do to us, and whether fiction can ever really capture the truth." These writer-characters frequently face crises of identity and creativity.

=== Themes ===Introducing the author at a national awards ceremony, a fellow writer said: "He once said that daily life is the frame that makes the picture. His commitment, as I see it, is to celebrate and explore the America we live in—the America that is right outside our door. He takes the common experiences of common Americans and transforms them into unforgettable fiction." The presenter went on: "While literary fiction often turns its gaze away from ordinary life, this author has embraced it. His characters work in gas stations and teach in public schools; they struggle with addiction and argue about money. He has never looked down on his characters or his readers. That is a rare and valuable gift." In his own acceptance remarks, the author said: "I've always believed that good fiction begins with story and progresses to theme. The best stories always end up being about the people rather than the events."

=== Influences ===In his memoir On the Craft, the author says "If you want to be a writer, you must do two things above all: read a lot and write a lot." He emphasizes the importance of good description, which "begins with clear seeing and ends with clear writing." He cautions against overwriting: "The key to good description begins with clear seeing and ends with clear writing, the kind of writing that employs fresh images and simple vocabulary." He is known for recommending that aspiring writers read widely across genres, not just within their own field.

=== Influences ===The author has called a classic science fiction writer "the author who influenced me most". Other influences include several prominent American and British novelists, short story writers, and literary figures from the mid-twentieth century. He has spoken about the influence of naturalist writers on his sense of place and setting. In interviews, he frequently discusses his love of crime fiction and its influence on his plotting. He says: "I think the best suspense fiction is literary fiction in disguise." He has also cited popular culture—particularly rock and roll music, B-movies, and television—as important influences, arguing that a writer who ignores popular culture is "cutting himself off from the main current of American experience."

=== Influences ===In a survey of favorite books, the author chose a mix of classics and genre fiction, including Adventures of Huckleberry Finn, Lord of the Flies, and several crime and horror novels. He has also praised a number of contemporary literary novelists. His reading tastes are notably eclectic: he reads mystery novels alongside works of literary fiction, and has championed genre writers whom he believes deserve wider recognition. He credits his mother with instilling his love of reading: "She would read to me every night, and when she wasn't reading to me, she was reading to herself."

=== Critical reception ===The author has been praised for his use of realistic detail. In an anthology of suspense fiction, the editor wrote that "While there were many good best-selling writers before him, he was really the first to use realistic settings, characters and situations in genre fiction to the extent that he has." A critic described his prose style as characterized by "an eye for the telling detail that other writers overlook." Another noted that "his gift lies in his ability to find the extraordinary in the ordinary, to take the stuff of everyday American life and invest it with dramatic significance." However, he has also faced criticism from some literary quarters for the perceived commercial nature of his work.

=== Critical reception ===Many critics argue that the author has matured as a writer. In an analysis of post–World War II genre fiction, a literary scholar devotes a chapter to the author in which he concludes: "He has a gift for narrative and a knack for characterization." The scholar notes that his later work shows "a growing sophistication and ambition that his earlier novels only hinted at." However, the scholar also argues that the author's best work is found in his shorter fiction, where "the discipline of limited space focuses his considerable talent."

=== Critical reception ===In 2003, the author was honored by the National Book Awards with a lifetime achievement award, the Medal of Distinguished Contribution to American Letters. Some in the literary community expressed disapproval. A literary critic said giving the award to a popular genre writer was "another low in the shocking process of dumbing down our cultural life." Others argued that the distinction between "literary" and "popular" fiction was artificial, and that the author's work had earned its place in American literature through its cultural impact and the quality of its best work.

=== Critical reception ===The author acknowledged the controversy in his acceptance speech: "There are some people who have spoken out passionately about giving me this medal. There are some people who think it's a terrible idea." He spoke of the gap between popular fiction and literary fiction, arguing it was harmful: "What do you think? You get social or academic brownie points for deliberately staying out of touch with your culture? There's a writer with a huge-selling series—not me—who said, 'I don't read popular fiction. I'm afraid it might compromise my style.' Well, the word for that kind of attitude is 'snobbery.'" He concluded with a plea for openness: "What I'm asking you to think about is whether the storytelling tradition—the popular tradition—has anything to offer us as writers and as readers. I think it does. I think it offers everything. The storytelling tradition is the oldest and most vital tradition in all of literature." His speech was widely covered and sparked a broader cultural conversation about the relationship between literary and popular fiction.

=== Critical reception ===A prominent film critic wrote that "A lot of people were outraged when he was honored at the National Book Awards, as if a popular writer couldn't be taken seriously. But after finding that a prison library's collection of his books was the most requested, I thought this was entirely appropriate. Genre or not, he has entered the consciousness of America. He is the literary voice of the baby boom generation." The critic's comments reflected a growing consensus that the author's cultural influence was undeniable regardless of literary classification.

=== Appraisal by other authors ===A literary novelist said that, upon giving a reading with the author, "It dawned on me as I listened to him that, never mind all the best sellers and all the stereotypes -- this was a true writer listening to, and speaking for, his audience. He's really very much a kindred soul to Dickens, in that way—he's writing for a big, general audience, and he takes their experience seriously." The observation drew parallels between the two authors' commitments to reaching the widest possible readership.

=== Appraisal by other authors ===A literary novelist praised the author's sense of place: "His fiction is famously saturated with the atmosphere of New England; much of his most vividly imagined work is set in small towns that feel both specific and universal. He has created a landscape of the imagination that is recognizably American." The praise highlighted his ability to turn regional settings into a coherent fictional universe.

=== Appraisal by other authors ===A fellow novelist compared the author favorably to Charles Dickens: "Both are novelists of vast popularity and enormous bibliographies, both are beloved writers with a pronounced social conscience and a genius for the vivid, physical rendering of character and scene." The comparison reflected a growing critical consensus about the author's place in the broader tradition of popular storytelling.

=== Appraisal by other authors ===A literary figure assigned two of the author's novels while teaching at a university. He praised the author's ear for dialogue: "He's one of the first people to recognize that young people have their own language and culture. He writes about teenagers and children with a respect and accuracy that most literary writers can't match." He described the author's work as having an emotional directness that much contemporary literary fiction lacks.

=== Influence ===In an interview, a fellow writer recalls the influence of the author, "who was always writing about underdogs, and bullied kids, and kids fighting back against overwhelming, often supernatural, forces. I mean, those kids were fighting against monsters and demons and ghosts, but that's what it felt like to be a kid from a difficult background." The observation highlighted the author's particular resonance with readers from marginalized communities.

=== Influence ===A literary novelist says that "I love this author and I owe him more than I could ever express... I love his wild imagination and his vivid scenes, many of which populate my nightmares even now. But what he really taught me, what I really carry with me, is the pacing—how to create that feeling of dread, that certainty that something terrible is about to happen, and then how to deliver on that promise." The novelist credits the author with demonstrating that genre fiction could be simultaneously entertaining and artistically ambitious.

=== Influence ===The hero of a Pulitzer Prize-winning novel dreams of being the next great popular novelist, and the novel's author alludes to his work several times throughout. Another National Book Award winner has praised the author's influence, saying: "We all owe him a debt. He made it possible for genre writers to be taken seriously." These references and tributes illustrate the extent to which the author's influence has permeated contemporary American literature, crossing boundaries between genre and literary fiction.

== Views and activism ==The author was raised in a Protestant denomination, but lost his belief in organized religion while in high school. While not conventionally religious, he says he chooses to believe in God.
== Views and activism ==In 1984, the author endorsed a Democratic presidential campaign.

== Views and activism ==In April 2008, the author spoke out against a bill pending in a state legislature that would restrict or ban the sale of violent video games to anyone under the age of 18. He argued that such legislation was a form of censorship that could have a chilling effect on creative expression. "The idea that video games cause violence is not supported by the evidence," he said. "We need to stop scapegoating the entertainment industry."
== Views and activism ==During the 2008 presidential election, the author endorsed the Democratic candidate.
== Views and activism ==On March 8, 2011, the author spoke at a political rally aimed against a state governor, voicing his opposition to a conservative political movement.

== Views and activism ==On April 30, 2012, the author published an article in a major news outlet calling for rich Americans, including himself, to pay more taxes, citing it as "a practical necessity and moral imperative." The article attracted widespread attention and was praised by progressive commentators.

== Views and activism ==On January 25, 2013, the author published an essay on gun control as a digital single, which discusses the gun debate in the wake of a school shooting. He called for reform of gun laws while acknowledging the complexity of the issue. The essay was praised for its directness and its willingness to engage with the arguments of gun rights advocates.

== Views and activism ==In 2016, the author was one of many writers who signed a letter condemning a political candidacy. It began: "Because, as writers, we are particularly aware of the many ways that language can be abused in the service of demagoguery and fear." The letter attracted widespread media coverage and was signed by hundreds of prominent American writers. The author was vocal about his opposition throughout the election season, using social media to express his views.
== Views and activism ==The author publicly criticized a politician who shared his surname, calling the politician a racist and saying he was tired of being confused with him.
== Views and activism ==In June 2018, the author called for the release of a jailed filmmaker, joining an international campaign by writers and artists.

== Views and activism ==In the 2020 Democratic Party presidential primaries, the author endorsed a progressive candidate's campaign. After that candidate suspended their campaign, the author later endorsed the eventual nominee.

== Views and activism ==In 2022, during an international conflict, the author expressed support for the country under invasion. On his social media account, the author posted photos showing his solidarity and later endorsed an aid campaign.

== Views and activism ==In July 2022, the author appeared in a video call with pranksters who impersonated a world leader. In the call the author expressed support for the besieged country and its people. The call went viral when the prank was revealed. The author later said he was embarrassed by the incident but stood by his statements of support.

== Views and activism ==The author testified in August 2022 in a case brought by the government to block a major publishing merger. He argued that consolidation in publishing would harm authors and reduce the diversity of books available to readers. His testimony was widely covered and helped shape public discourse about the case.
=== State politics ===The author endorsed a candidate in a 2014 U.S. Senate election.

=== State politics ===The author publicly criticized a state governor during his tenure, calling him out for cuts to education and social services. He used social media and public appearances to voice his opposition. The author said: "This governor does not represent the values of this state." His criticism attracted national media attention.

=== State politics ===The attention garnered by the political criticism led to efforts to encourage the author to run for governor. He said he would not run or serve. He later posted on social media supporting another candidate in the gubernatorial race.

== Philanthropy ==The author subsidizes a literary foundation and has endowed scholarships at his alma mater. He has also given generously to local libraries and reading programs. In 2000, the author and his wife founded a charitable foundation to support literacy, education, and the arts. The foundation has donated millions of dollars to schools, fire departments, and community organizations. In 2014, the author donated $1 million to a local library, and in 2012 he funded a new Little League baseball field in his hometown.

== Philanthropy ==In 2002, the author and several other prominent writers organized a benefit reading to raise funds for a seriously injured colleague. The event raised over $100,000 and brought together some of the most prominent voices in American literature.

== Philanthropy ==In November 2011, the author's foundation donated $70,000 in matched funding via his radio station to help pay the heating bills for families in need in his hometown during a difficult winter.
== Philanthropy ==In February 2021, the author's foundation donated $6,500 to help children from an elementary school publish two novels on which they had been working over the past year.

== Personal life ==The author married Margaret Morris on January 2, 1971. She is also a novelist and philanthropist. They own and divide their time between three houses: one in their home state, one in the mountains, and one in Florida. Their primary residence features a distinctive wrought-iron gate with decorative motifs. Visitors and fans frequently stop to photograph the gate.

== Personal life ==The couple have three children—two sons and a daughter. Their eldest son is also a published novelist who writes under a pen name. Their younger son has collaborated with the author on several books. Their daughter is a minister. The family has strong creative ties; the author has spoken about the influence of his children on his later work.

== Personal life ==The author is a longtime fan of baseball, particularly his local team. In 1990, he published an essay about his son's Little League team in a national magazine. The author and a friend co-authored a book about being devoted fans. "Baseball is the game that's closest to life," the author has said. "It's a game of failure—even the best hitters fail seven out of ten times."

== Personal life ==The author and his wife own a radio station group consisting of several local stations. Music, particularly rock, plays a role in much of his work. On the subject of music, the author says: "I've always written to rock and roll. I need the noise. When I write, I listen to bands like AC/DC and Metallica and the Ramones." He has included references to songs and musicians throughout his fiction, and has described the relationship between music and writing as "two different expressions of the same creative impulse." He has also spoken about his love of audiobooks and has narrated several of his own works.

== Personal life ==The author remains a voracious reader. When asked about his reading habits, he replied, "I'm sort of an omnivore, apt to go from the latest crime novel to classic literature to contemporary fiction to nonfiction in the space of a few weeks." He says he reads between seventy and eighty books a year. His reading choices are eclectic: he has praised both literary novelists and genre writers, and has been an outspoken advocate for reading as a fundamental human activity. "Books are a uniquely portable magic," he has said.

=== Accident and aftermath ===On a summer day in 1999, at about 4:30 p.m., the author was walking on the shoulder of a rural road. A driver, distracted by an unrestrained dog in his vehicle, struck the author. The author was thrown fourteen feet and suffered a collapsed right lung, multiple fractures of his right leg, a scalp laceration, and a broken hip. Witnesses described the scene as horrific. Emergency services arrived within minutes.

=== Accident and aftermath ===The author was conscious enough to give a deputy phone numbers to contact his family but was in considerable pain. He was transported to a hospital where he underwent emergency surgery. Over the following weeks, he endured five operations and months of physical therapy. He was bedridden for much of the summer of 1999 and was told he might never walk normally again. He later wrote about the experience in his memoir, noting that the pain was "beyond anything I had ever imagined."

=== Accident and aftermath ===The author's wife arranged to purchase the vehicle that struck him, reportedly to prevent it from appearing on the internet as a morbid curiosity. He recalls: "When I was in the hospital, mostly unconscious, my wife Maggie already had a plan. She bought the van. She said she wanted to make sure nobody would ever sell it as a souvenir." The author later used the experience as material for his writing, exploring themes of vulnerability and mortality that became more prominent in his later work. The driver who struck him died a year later of an apparent overdose.

=== Appearances in other media ===In a popular novel, a famous author writes that the fictional protagonist is working on an adaptation of another writer's book. The reference is played for humor and reflects the author's prominent place in American popular culture. He has been referenced in dozens of novels, films, and television shows, usually as a shorthand for mainstream commercial success or the horror genre more broadly.

=== Appearances in other media ===In 1988, a rock band recorded an updated version of one of its classic songs; the single released for radio play featured a narrative intro spoken by the author. He has also appeared in music videos and has provided voice work for animated projects.

=== Appearances in other media ===The author was a contestant on a popular television quiz show in 1995 and 1998. He has made cameos in adaptations of his work and appeared as a character in various television shows. He directed a number of television episodes. In interviews, he has said that he enjoys these appearances because they allow him to "step out from behind the typewriter."
== Awards and honors ==His debut novel was included on a major library's list of Books of the Century under the category "Pop Culture Mass & Entertainment".

== Awards and honors ==In 2008, his memoir was ranked 21st on a major entertainment magazine's list of "The New Classics: The 100 Best Reads from 1983 to 2008". It also made a prominent news magazine's list of the 100 greatest nonfiction books of all time. In 2020, the memoir was ranked by an international newspaper as one of the greatest nonfiction books ever written.

== Awards and honors ==His 2011 novel was named one of the five best fiction books of the year by The New York Times: "Throughout his career, he has explored fresh ways to blend the ordinary and the apocalyptic. His approach is both deeply literary and unmistakably popular." The novel won several major book awards and was later adapted into a television series.
